tathƒÅgato.summary: An epithet of the Buddha with a deliberate double derivation: ‚Äúthus gone‚Äù and ‚Äúthus come‚Äù.
breakdown: Gone (past participle of gacchati): having gone the path to enlightenment from beginning to end.

tathƒÅgato.goenka: TathƒÅgato is the term the Buddha uses whenever he talks about himself. The tradition accepts both derivations: from tathƒÅ gata, ‚Äúhaving gone thus‚Äù ‚Äî having gone the path to enlightenment from the beginning to the end ‚Äî and from tathƒÅ ƒÅgata, ‚Äúhaving come thus‚Äù ‚Äî having attained the state of enlightenment by the path he and all the Buddhas have proclaimed. A translation that picks only one of these loses the intended double meaning, so the term is usually left untranslated as ‚Äúthe TathƒÅgata‚Äù.

ta·πáhƒÅ.meaning: craving; the deep mental thirst that drives the cycle of suffering
breakdown: Root meaning ‚Äúto thirst‚Äù; ta·πáhƒÅ is literally ‚Äúthirst‚Äù, used for the deep mental thirst of craving.
ta·πáhƒÅ.goenka: Ta·πáhƒÅ ‚Äî craving ‚Äî is the central diagnostic of the Four Noble Truths: the second truth identifies it as the origin of suffering. The tradition explains the chain by which it arises: consciousness (vi√±√±ƒÅ·πáa) arises with the impact on the sense organs, contact (samphassa) follows, producing sensations (samphassajƒÅ vedanƒÅ) that are cognized as enticing and pleasurable; perception (sa√±√±ƒÅ) is followed by a mental reaction (sa√±cetanƒÅ) which results in craving (ta·πáhƒÅ) towards an increase of objects that may continue the process of enjoyment. Three kinds are distinguished: kƒÅmata·πáhƒÅ (craving for sensual pleasures), bhavata·πáhƒÅ (craving for becoming, for continued existence) and vibhavata·πáhƒÅ (craving for non-becoming, annihilation). Its defining characteristic is ponobbhavikƒÅ nandƒ´rƒÅgasahagatƒÅ tatratatrƒÅbhinandinƒ´ ‚Äî leading to renewed becoming, accompanied by delight and lust, seeking delight here and there. NibbƒÅna itself is defined as ta·πáhƒÅkkhayo, the annihilation of craving.
ta·πáhƒÅ.note: divergence: not mere ‚Äúdesire‚Äù or ‚Äúwanting‚Äù (too weak), nor literal ‚Äúthirst‚Äù alone ‚Äî ta·πáhƒÅ is the deep mental thirst that perpetuates suffering.
